Gaussian/Eisenstein integer norms (sum of two squares / $x^2+xy+y^2$) - Buffed Seed

Let p=1000249 and q=100003. These are distinct odd primes with p\equiv 1\pmod 4 and q\equiv 3\pmod 4.

Let (x,y) be the unique integers with x>y>0 and x^2+y^2=p.

Let mode = coupling_delta_1, so \delta=1.

Define t to be the least positive residue of x\cdot y^{-1}\pmod p; i.e. 1\le t\le p-1 and t\equiv x\,y^{-1}\pmod p.

Define the q-periodic sequence (a_n)_{n\ge 0} by
a_n\equiv n^2+t\,n+(t+\delta)\pmod q.

Assume the non-degeneracy condition q\nmid\bigl(t^2-4(t+\delta)\bigr). Let
N:=|\{0\le n\le q-1 : a_n\equiv 0\pmod q\}|.

Compute A, the unique integer with 0\le A<1000 such that
A\equiv x+2y+5N+\delta\pmod{1000}.

Solution.
Step 1 (unique_factorization_Zi_optional): By the sum-of-two-squares criterion, an odd prime p is representable as p=x^2+y^2 in integers iff p\equiv 1\pmod 4, equivalently p splits in \mathbb{Z}[i] as p=\pi\bar\pi with N(\pi)=p. Since p=1000249\equiv 1\pmod 4, such integers x,y exist with \gcd(x,y)=1. Because p is odd prime, exactly one of x,y is even, so \gcd(y,p)=1 and y^{-1}\pmod p is well-defined.

Step 2 (descent_via_norms_optional): Compute 13p-1=13\cdot 1000249-1=13003236=3606^2, hence u:=3606 satisfies u^2\equiv -1\pmod p. Apply Euclidean descent (Cornacchia descent) to (p,u):
1000249 = 3606\cdot 277 + 1387,
3606 = 1387\cdot 2 + 832,
1387 = 832\cdot 1 + 555.
Here 832^2<p and p-832^2=308025=555^2, so p=832^2+555^2.

Step 3 (roots_of_unity_filter_light): The only units in \mathbb{Z}[i] are \{\pm 1,\pm i\}; multiplying x+iy by a unit or conjugating only changes signs and swaps x,y. Thus for a prime p\equiv 1\pmod 4, the positive representations of p as x^2+y^2 are unique up to swapping x,y, and the normalization x>y>0 chooses exactly one. Using the existence and coprimality from Step 1 together with the explicit pair from Step 2, the normalized pair is (x,y)=(832,555).

Step 4 (norm_to_modular_interface): Reduce x^2+y^2=p modulo p to get x^2\equiv -y^2\pmod p. Since \gcd(y,p)=1 from Step 1, multiply by (y^{-1})^2 to obtain (x\,y^{-1})^2\equiv -1\pmod p. Therefore any t\equiv x\,y^{-1}\pmod p satisfies t^2\equiv -1\pmod p.

Step 5 (norm_to_modular_interface): Compute y^{-1}\pmod p and hence t. The Euclidean algorithm gives
1000249 = 555\cdot 1802 + 139,
555 = 139\cdot 3 + 138,
139 = 138\cdot 1 + 1,
so 1 = 1000249\cdot 4 - 555\cdot 7209. Hence 555^{-1}\equiv -7209\pmod p. Using (x,y) from Step 3 and the definition from Step 4,
t\equiv 832\cdot (-7209)\equiv 3606\pmod p,
and 1\le 3606\le p-1, so t=3606.

Step 6 (quadratic_congruence_root_count): Over \mathbb{F}_q with odd prime q, the congruence n^2+bn+c\equiv 0\pmod q has 1+\left(\frac{b^2-4c}{q}\right) solutions in \{0,1,\dots,q-1\}, because completing the square shows solvability depends on whether the discriminant is a quadratic residue. Applying this with b=t and c=t+\delta gives
N = 1+\left(\frac{D}{q}\right), where D:=t^2-4(t+\delta).
By the non-degeneracy assumption, D\not\equiv 0\pmod q, so \left(\frac{D}{q}\right)\in\{\pm 1\} and N\in\{0,2\}.

Step 7 (quadratic_reciprocity_legendre_symbol_compute): Using t=3606 from Step 5 and \delta=1, compute
D\equiv 3606^2-4(3606+1)=12988808\equiv 88421\pmod{100003}.
Factor 88421=29\cdot 3049, so
\left(\frac{D}{q}\right)=\left(\frac{29}{q}\right)\left(\frac{3049}{q}\right).

Step 8 (quadratic_reciprocity_legendre_symbol_compute): Evaluate \left(\frac{29}{q}\right) for q=100003. Since 29\equiv 1\pmod 4, quadratic reciprocity gives \left(\frac{29}{q}\right)=\left(\frac{q}{29}\right). Now q\equiv 11\pmod{29}, so \left(\frac{29}{q}\right)=\left(\frac{11}{29}\right)=\left(\frac{29\bmod 11}{11}\right)=\left(\frac{7}{11}\right). Because 7\equiv 3\pmod 4 and 11\equiv 3\pmod 4, reciprocity gives \left(\frac{7}{11}\right)=-\left(\frac{11\bmod 7}{7}\right)=-\left(\frac{4}{7}\right)=-1. Hence \left(\frac{29}{q}\right)=-1.

Step 9 (quadratic_reciprocity_legendre_symbol_compute): Evaluate \left(\frac{3049}{q}\right). Since 3049\equiv 1\pmod 4, reciprocity gives \left(\frac{3049}{q}\right)=\left(\frac{q}{3049}\right). Compute q\equiv 2435\pmod{3049}, so
\left(\frac{3049}{q}\right)=\left(\frac{2435}{3049}\right)=\left(\frac{5}{3049}\right)\left(\frac{487}{3049}\right).
Now \left(\frac{5}{3049}\right)=\left(\frac{3049\bmod 5}{5}\right)=\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)=1.
Also \left(\frac{487}{3049}\right)=\left(\frac{3049\bmod 487}{487}\right)=\left(\frac{127}{487}\right), and since 127\equiv 3\pmod 4 and 487\equiv 3\pmod 4,
\left(\frac{127}{487}\right)=-\left(\frac{487\bmod 127}{127}\right)=-\left(\frac{106}{127}\right).
Write 106=2\cdot 53. The supplementary law gives \left(\frac{2}{127}\right)=1 because 127\equiv 7\pmod 8, and reciprocity gives \left(\frac{53}{127}\right)=\left(\frac{127\bmod 53}{53}\right)=\left(\frac{21}{53}\right).
Finally \left(\frac{21}{53}\right)=\left(\frac{3}{53}\right)\left(\frac{7}{53}\right), where \left(\frac{3}{53}\right)=\left(\frac{53\bmod 3}{3}\right)=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)=-1 and \left(\frac{7}{53}\right)=\left(\frac{53\bmod 7}{7}\right)=\left(\frac{4}{7}\right)=1. Hence \left(\frac{21}{53}\right)=-1, so \left(\frac{53}{127}\right)=-1 and \left(\frac{106}{127}\right)=-1. Therefore \left(\frac{127}{487}\right)=1, so \left(\frac{487}{3049}\right)=1 and \left(\frac{3049}{q}\right)=1.

Step 10 (quadratic_reciprocity_legendre_symbol_compute): Combine the evaluations from Step 8 and from Step 9 with the factorization from Step 7 to get
\left(\frac{D}{q}\right)=(-1)\cdot 1=-1.
Therefore from Step 6, N=1+\left(\frac{D}{q}\right)=0.

Step 11 (roots_of_unity_filter_light): Using (x,y)=(832,555) from Step 3 and N=0 from Step 10 with \delta=1,
A\equiv x+2y+5N+\delta \equiv 832+2\cdot 555+0+1=1943\equiv 943\pmod{1000}.
With 0\le A<1000, A=943.
Answer: 943
3) Uniqueness Test
- Uniqueness of (x,y): For p\equiv 1\pmod 4 prime, p splits in \mathbb{Z}[i] as p=\pi\bar\pi and the only symmetries of x+iy are multiplication by \{\pm 1,\pm i\} and conjugation, which only changes signs and swaps x,y. Thus there is exactly one pair with x>y>0; Step 3 fixes (x,y)=(832,555).

- Uniqueness of t: Since \gcd(y,p)=1, the congruence t\equiv x\,y^{-1}\pmod p has a unique solution modulo p; requiring 1\le t\le p-1 makes t a unique integer. Step 5 gives t=3606.

- Uniqueness of N: By Step 6, N=1+\left(\frac{D}{q}\right) with D=t^2-4(t+\delta), and the non-degeneracy assumption gives D\not\equiv 0\pmod q, so N\in\{0,2\}. Steps 7-10 compute \left(\frac{D}{q}\right)=-1, forcing N=0.

- Uniqueness of A: A is defined by a single congruence modulo 1000 and the range 0\le A<1000 selects exactly one value. Step 11 yields A=943.

- Collision resistance: the only other admissible root-count branch is N=2, which would give A\equiv 832+1110+10+1\equiv 953\pmod{1000}, different from 943.

943
